edges.description: Microvascular sequestration contributes to BBB injury and neurologic manifestations.
edges.target: Blood-brain barrier dysfunction in cerebral malaria

edges.description: Barrier dysfunction and microvascular pathology contribute to cerebral edema and coma.
edges.source: Blood-brain barrier dysfunction in cerebral malaria

edges.description: Cerebral vascular and inflammatory injury contributes to seizure risk.
edges.source: Blood-brain barrier dysfunction in cerebral malaria

edges.description: Persistence of hypnozoites drives repeated blood-stage episodes.

edges.source: Primaquine radical cure for vivax malaria

nodes.description: Cerebral malaria involves endothelial barrier breakdown with increased permeability and stress responses, linking vascular pathology to severe neurologic phenotypes.
nodes.id: Blood-brain barrier dysfunction in cerebral malaria

evidence.snippet: Blood–brain barrier (BBB) disruption is a central feature of cerebral malaria (CM), a severe complication of Plasmodium falciparum (Pf) infections.
After 6-h of co-culture with Pf-iRBCs, hiPSC-BMECs showed reduced TEER and increased sodium fluorescein permeability compared to co-culture with uninfected RBCs, indicative of a leaky barrier.

nodes.description: Cerebral malaria can present with coma as a defining severe neurologic manifestation.

evidence.snippet: The disease is defined clinically as an otherwise unexplained coma in someone with malaria parasitemia.

nodes.description: Dormant P. vivax hypnozoites in the liver sustain recurrent malaria episodes unless eradicated with radical cure.

evidence.reference_title: Effect of primaquine dose on the risk of recurrence in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax: a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Primaquine is used to eliminate Plasmodium vivax hypnozoites, but its optimal dosing regimen remains unclear.

nodes.description: In severe falciparum malaria, parasitized erythrocytes sequester in the microvasculature, promoting endothelial injury, impaired perfusion, and downstream organ dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: EPCR and Malaria Severity: The Center of a Perfect Storm.
evidence.snippet: Infected erythrocyte sequestration in the microvasculature plays a critical role in the development of severe disease

nodes.description: Primaquine reduces recurrent vivax malaria by targeting dormant liver hypnozoites.
nodes.id: Primaquine radical cure for vivax malaria

evidence.reference_title: Effect of primaquine dose on the risk of recurrence in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax: a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Compared with treatment without primaquine, the rate of P vivax recurrence was lower after treatment with low-dose primaquine (adjusted hazard ratio 0·21, 95% CI 0·17-0·27; p<0·0001) and high-dose primaquine (0·10, 0·08-0·12; p<0·0001).

evidence.reference_title: Effect of primaquine dose on the risk of recurrence in patients with uncomplicated Plasmodium vivax: a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: At day 180, the risk of recurrence was 51·0% (95% CI 48·2-53·9) in 1470 patients treated without primaquine, 19·3% (16·9-21·9) in 2569 patients treated with a low total dose of primaquine (approximately 3·5 mg/kg), and 8·1% (7·0-9·4) in 2811 patients treated with a high total dose of primaquine (approximately 7 mg/kg), regardless of primaquine treatment duration.

nodes.description: Seizures are a major severe neurologic manifestation in cerebral malaria.

evidence.snippet: Acute seizures are common and increasing numbers of them during the index illness are associated with a greater likelihood of adverse outcomes.
